text.title: AI IaC Hallucination Detector Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every hallucination detector enforces. Hard rules; the schema is the authoritative ground truth.

rule.statement: The provider schema (from Terraform Registry / Pulumi registry / chart values.schema.json) is the SOLE source of truth for argument names, types, and required-vs-optional. Memory-based or LLM-generated "the API also accepts X" is REJECTED.
rule.rationale: AI assistants confidently emit arguments that the schema does not contain. Without schema validation, the misfiring lands as a runtime error during `terraform plan` or — worse — as a no-op argument that silently ignores intent.

rule.statement: Schema validation MUST run against the EXACT provider version pinned in the repo's `required_providers` / chart Chart.yaml. Unpinned providers (RF-03 from the red-flags sibling) MUST be flagged and the detector MUST refuse to emit a schema-clean verdict until the pin is added.
rule.rationale: `aws` provider v5.40 has different fields from v4.x. Validating v5 code against v4 schema produces false positives; validating v4 code against v5 schema produces false negatives. Pin is non-negotiable for accurate validation.

rule.statement: The detector MUST use a real AST parser (tree-sitter HCL, helm-go-template AST, hcl2 SDK), NOT regex or string match. Regex emits false positives on commented arguments and false negatives on heredoc / interpolation. AST parsing is non-optional.
rule.rationale: HCL syntax allows the same argument to appear in valid and invalid forms depending on block context. Regex cannot model context; AST can. Validating with regex is a known dead end.

rule.statement: Deprecated arguments are flagged as a distinct class (`deprecation`), not "hallucination". The detector MUST emit the replacement field name from the schema's `deprecation_message` where present. Treating deprecation as hallucination misleads the reviewer.
rule.rationale: Terraform providers maintain a deprecation horizon — fields remain accepted for one major version with a warning. Conflating deprecation with hallucination forces unnecessary rewrites; treating them distinctly lets the team plan deprecation cleanup.

rule.statement: Any LLM-suggested fix MUST be re-validated against the schema before being emitted in the PR comment. The detector MUST NOT trust the LLM's claim that the replacement is correct; it MUST run the same schema validation on the suggested fix.
rule.rationale: Fix-suggestion routing tends to hallucinate plausible-but-wrong replacements ("use `aws_s3_bucket_v2` instead", which does not exist). Re-validation breaks the chain.

example.title: Schema-validated detection + schema-validated fix
example.code:
Detection (v5.40 schema): resource "aws_s3_bucket" "logs" .acl = "private" # NOT in schema (hallucination)
Suggested fix (schema-validated): Move "acl" to a separate aws_s3_bucket_acl resource: resource "aws_s3_bucket_acl" "logs" { bucket = aws_s3_bucket.logs.id acl = "private" } Verified: aws_s3_bucket_acl resource exists in v5.40 schema with required arguments bucket, acl.
example.note: r1, r3, r5 satisfied. Schema reference grounds both detection and fix.

example.title: LLM-suggested non-existent replacement
example.code: Detection: aws_s3_bucket.acl deprecated/removed in v5. Suggested fix (LLM-only, not validated): resource "aws_s3_bucket_v2" "logs" { ... } # does NOT exist
example.note: r5 violation. The detector emitted a fix without re-validating against the schema. Reviewer follows the suggestion, terraform plan fails, time wasted.
